sourceMovie.title: 48 Hrs.
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto 48 Hrs. (1982) as a crime theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The crime and action blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Hot Fuzz
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Hot Fuzz (2007) is the natural next step after 48 Hrs.: same crime DNA, different perspective. The funny streak and action elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Beverly Hills Cop
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of 48 Hrs. who respond to its crime will find Beverly Hills Cop (1984) immediately familiar. Both films share a action sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Rush Hour 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason 48 Hrs. fans keep landing on Rush Hour 2 (2001) — the crime is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Rush Hour 3
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Rush Hour 3 draws from the same crime well as 48 Hrs., channeling action in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2007 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Tango & Cash
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Tango & Cash (1989) captures the crime appeal of 48 Hrs. without simply imitating it. The action streak running through both films is what puts Tango & Cash at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Lethal Weapon
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The crime appeal of 48 Hrs. — that dark edge, the action — translates cleanly into Lethal Weapon (1987). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Lethal Weapon 3
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Lethal Weapon 3 (1992) clearly understood what makes crime work — the same quality that keeps 48 Hrs. rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Magnum Force
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Magnum Force (1973) is the kind of film 48 Hrs. fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The crime overlap is real, but it's the tense emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Dragged Across Concrete
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Dragged Across Concrete shares 48 Hrs.'s gift for crime — the 2019 film delivers action without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Sudden Impact
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes 48 Hrs. compelling — the crime, the action — shows up in Sudden Impact (1983) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that dark storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

faq.question: What should I watch after 48 Hrs.?
faq.answer: Try Hot Fuzz, Beverly Hills Cop, Rush Hour 2—each shares DNA with 48 Hrs. while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is 48 Hrs.?
faq.answer: 48 Hrs. (1982) is best described as Crime, Action, Comedy. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is 48 Hrs. part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "48 Hrs. Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
